label: Representation Collapse
definition: A failure mode in which a model's learned representations lose diversity and collapse toward a single point or a low-dimensional subspace, so different inputs map to nearly the same embedding and the representation stops being useful.

body.text: Representation collapse is what happens when a model learns to throw away the variation it was supposed to preserve. Instead of mapping different inputs to distinct, informative points in latent-space, the encoder sends many or all inputs to the same vector, or to a thin subspace far smaller than the space it has available. In the most extreme case every input produces an identical output, a constant representation that carries no information about which input it came from. The representation has collapsed, and any downstream task that depends on telling inputs apart fails, because the distinctions it needs were erased upstream.

The failure is most associated with self-supervised and contrastive-learning methods, where it is a built-in temptation. These methods train an encoder by pulling together the representations of related inputs, for example two augmentations of the same image. If only that attraction is present, there is a trivial solution: map everything to one point, which makes every pair perfectly close and the loss zero, while learning nothing. Preventing this is the central design problem of the field. Contrastive methods add a repulsive force, pushing apart the representations of unrelated inputs so a constant solution is penalized; non-contrastive methods avoid collapse instead through architectural asymmetries, stop-gradients, or explicit terms that decorrelate the feature dimensions and keep the representation spread out.

Collapse comes in degrees, and naming them helps. Complete collapse maps all inputs to one vector. The subtler and more common dimensional collapse keeps the representations distinct but confines them to a low-dimensional subspace, so most of the embedding's capacity goes unused and whole directions carry no signal; you can diagnose it by inspecting the singular value spectrum of the representations and seeing many values near zero. A related and well-studied endpoint in supervised classification is neural collapse, where late in training the features of each class concentrate tightly around their class mean in a highly symmetric arrangement. The information-bottleneck view frames the danger precisely: compress too aggressively and the representation keeps too few bits about the input to remain useful.

Representation collapse matters because so much of modern machine learning rests on the quality of learned representations. An embedding-model whose space has partially collapsed yields weaker retrieval and clustering, since genuinely different items sit too close together. The closely related mode collapse in a generative-adversarial-network is the generative cousin, where the generator covers only a few modes of the data instead of its full diversity. Collapse also shadows knowledge-distillation and other representation-transfer methods, where a degenerate teacher or objective can pass on an impoverished space. Detecting it early, through embedding diversity, feature correlations, or the rank of the representation, and designing objectives that actively resist it, is a standard part of building systems that learn good representations rather than convenient but empty ones.

image.alt: A line plot of the log of the singular values of the covariance matrix of embedding vectors against the singular value rank index from 0 to about 128. The curve sits near a log value of minus four across the first ninety ranks, then falls off a cliff to about minus twenty-five, so a block of dimensions has near-zero variance.
image.caption: Representation collapse shows up in the singular value spectrum of the embeddings. A healthy representation spreads variance across many dimensions, but here a whole block of singular values drops to near zero (the cliff past rank ninety), meaning those dimensions carry no information and the embeddings have collapsed onto a lower-dimensional subspace. This narrow form is called dimensional collapse.
image.credit: Li Jing, Pascal Vincent, Yann LeCun, Yuandong Tian
